perturbation.original_text: For the purpose of this Agreement “ Confidential Information ” means any and all information, disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party, whether in oral or in written form, including but not limited to documentation, technic al or business information, ideas, plans, drawings, models and rights:of:way, requirements, s tandards, financial and non:financial data, marketing, trade secrets, know:how, customer and t enants lists, prices, as well as any and all intellectual and industrial property rights contain ed therein and/or in relation thereto; provided however, that Confidential Information shall not in clude information which (i) is or becomes lawfully in the public domain other than through a breach of any non disclosure agreement or any confidentiality obligation, (ii) was known to the Receiving Party prior to the disclosure, as evidenced by it, (iii) was independently develope d by or for the Receiving Party without reference to or use of Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party, (iv) was lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a third party without v iolation of a confidentiality obligation, (v) The Disclosing Party agrees in writing that it may be d isclosed by the Receiving Party, or (vi) is require d or compelled by law to be disclosed, provided that the Receiving Party gives reasonable prior written notice to the Disclosing Party to allow it to seek protective or other court orders.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purpose of this Agreement “ Confidential Information ” means any and all information, disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party, whether in oral or in written form. provided however, that Confidential Information shall not in clude information which (i) is or becomes lawfully in the public domain other than through a breach of any non disclosure agreement or any confidentiality obligation, (ii) was known to the Receiving Party prior to the disclosure, as evidenced by it, (iii) was independently develope d by or for the Receiving Party without reference to or use of Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party, (iv) was lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a third party without v iolation of a confidentiality obligation, (v) The Disclosing Party agrees in writing that it may be d isclosed by the Receiving Party, or (vi) is require d or compelled by law to be disclosed, provided that the Receiving Party gives reasonable prior written notice to the Disclosing Party to allow it to seek protective or other court orders.
perturbation.explanation: By removing specific examples of what constitutes 'Confidential Information' such as 'documentation, technical or business information, ideas, plans, drawings, models and rights:of:way, requirements, standards, financial and non:financial data, marketing, trade secrets, know:how, customer and tenants lists, prices, as well as any and all intellectual and industrial property rights contain ed therein and/or in relation thereto', the definition becomes vague. This creates an in-text contradiction when Section 5 refers to returning 'all copies or extracts of the Disclosing Party’s Confid ential Information' because it is now unclear what specifically needs to be returned or destroyed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Immediately upon the decision by either party not t o enter into the transaction or agreement contemplated by the parties hereto, and based on th e Disclosing Party's written request, the Receiving Party shall either destroy and certify su ch destruction or return to the Disclosing Party al l copies or extracts of the Disclosing Party’s Confid ential Information, in any medium and destruct it. Such complete return or destruction will be promptl y and duly confirmed in writing to the Disclosing Party or its proxy by the Receiving Part y.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a detailed list of examples that would qualify as "Confidential Information." The changed text removes those specific examples, making the definition of "Confidential Information" more vague. This creates a direct contradiction with the "Contradicted Text" section, which refers to returning "all copies or extracts of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information." Because the definition of 'Confidential Information' is now less specific due to the omission, it is unclear what the Receiving Party is obligated to return or destroy, directly conflicting with the requirement of the contradicted text. The perturbed text directly undermines the ability to enforce the return or destruction of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Each party may disclose the other party ’s Confidential Information to such party’s employees, if any, on a need:to:know basis only. The Receiving Party may disclose confidential information to professional a dvisors, including lawyers, accountants and board members (herein called " Permitted Persons ") only after the written consent of the Disclosing Party and only after it shall procur e that all of its Permitted Persons shall execute in writing at least a like obligation of co nfidentiality with respect to the confidential information and shall comply with the provisions hereof on the same terms and conditions as a Receiving Party, and such oblig ation will be handed to the Disclosing Party or its proxy .
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may disclose the other party’s Confidential Information to such party’s employees, if any, on a need:to:know basis only. The Receiving Party may disclose confidential information to professional advisors, including lawyers, accountants and board members only after the written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for 'Permitted Persons' (lawyers, accountants, board members) to execute a written confidentiality agreement, it creates a contradiction with Section 6, which states that 'Each party acknowledges that each of such terms and conditions is necessary to preserve the confidentiality of the information provided to it'. If Permitted Persons are not bound by a written agreement, the 'terms and conditions' are not fully preserved, leading to an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees to abide by each and ev ery term and condition set forth herein. Each party acknowledges that each of such terms and cond itions is necessary to preserve the confidentiality of the information provided to it a nd that a breach of any of the terms and conditions hereof will result in irreparable damage to the Dis closing Party /parties in an amount now impossible to calculate and, thereof, in the event of such breach, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled (in an addition to any other rights and r emedies it may have at law or in equity) to have an injunction issued by any court of competent jurisdi ction enjoining it and any other person involved therein from communing such breach.
perturbation.justification: The original text required 'Permitted Persons' to execute a written confidentiality agreement. The changed text removes this requirement. The contradicted text states that 'each of such terms and conditions is necessary to preserve the confidentiality of the information'. Removing the confidentiality agreement for 'Permitted Persons' directly contradicts the contradicted text, as the 'terms and conditions' are not fully preserved, leading to an in-text contradiction. This aligns with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may not make additional copies of the confidential information without the Disclosing Party's prior consent to be given on a c ase by case basis.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may make additional copies of the confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for the Disclosing Party's consent for additional copies, this creates an in-text contradiction with Section 3, which states 'All Confidential Information of a Disclosing Party is and shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party'. If the Receiving Party can freely make copies, it diminishes the Disclosing Party's property rights over the information, and Section 6, which states that 'Each party acknowledges that each of such terms and conditions is necessary to preserve the confidentiality of the information provided to it'. If the Receiving Party can freely make copies, it diminishes the confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidentiality of information All Confidential Information of a Disclosing Party is and shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall b e construed as granting or conferring any rights by license or otherwise, either express, implied or by estoppel, to any Confidential Information of a Disclosing Party, or under any patent, copyright, t rademark or trade secret of the Disclosing Party. 3The Receiving Party may make additional copies of the confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the Disclosing Party's consent for the Receiving Party to make additional copies of confidential information. The changed text removes this requirement, allowing the Receiving Party to freely make additional copies. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states, 'All Confidential Information of a Disclosing Party is and shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party,' and that 'Nothing contained in this Agreement shall b e construed as granting or conferring any rights by license or otherwise... to any Confidential Information of a Disclosing Party...' The changed text grants the Receiving Party more rights over the confidential information, diminishing the Disclosing Party's property rights, and thus, there is an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party make any warranties, e xpress or implied, regarding the accuracy, completeness, performance, merchantability, fitness for use, non:infringement of third parties, rights, or other attributes of its respective confi dential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party makes any warranties, express or implied, regarding the accuracy or completeness of its respective confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'performance, merchantability, fitness for use, non:infringement of third parties, rights, or other attributes' from the disclaimer of warranties, the contract implicitly suggests that some level of assurance *is* given regarding these aspects. This contradicts the 'As Is' provision in the same section. If the confidential information performs poorly or infringes on third-party rights, the Receiving Party could argue that the limited warranty creates an expectation of some level of performance or non-infringement, creating an ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Non: reliance All Confidential Information furnished under this agreement will be provided by the Disclosing Party "As Is". Neither party makes any warranties, express or implied, regarding the accuracy or completeness of its respective confidential information. 5. Withdrawal from the agreement Immediately upon the decision by either party not t o enter into the transaction or agreement contemplated by the parties hereto, and based on th e Disclosing Party's written request, the Receiving Party shall either destroy and certify su ch destruction or return to the Disclosing Party al l copies or extracts of the Disclosing Party’s Confid ential Information, in any medium and destruct it. Such complete return or destruction will be promptl y and duly confirmed in writing to the Disclosing Party or its proxy by the Receiving Part y.
perturbation.justification: The original text disclaimed all warranties, express or implied, including performance, merchantability, fitness for use, and non-infringement. The changed text removes these specific disclaimers, implying that some level of assurance *is* given regarding these aspects. The contradicted text states that the Confidential Information is provided "As Is" with no warranties. By removing the specific disclaimers, the changed text contradicts the "As Is" provision and creates an ambiguity regarding the level of assurance provided for the confidential information, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction as it breaks compatibility between the modified text and contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to Confidential Information received prior to termination, will survive for a period of three (3) years following the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to Confidential Information received prior to termination, will survive termination.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'three (3) years following the Effective Date' limitation, the obligation to protect confidential information after termination becomes perpetual. This contradicts the termination clause (Section 12) which implies a defined endpoint for the agreement and its obligations and Section 5's instruction to destroy or return Confidential Information. If obligations are perpetual, the act of destroying information might not fully absolve the Receiving Party of its responsibilities, creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Immediately upon the decision by either party not t o enter into the transaction or agreement contemplated by the parties hereto, and based on th e Disclosing Party's written request, the Receiving Party shall either destroy and certify su ch destruction or return to the Disclosing Party al l copies or extracts of the Disclosing Party’s Confid ential Information, in any medium and destruct it. Such complete return or destruction will be promptl y and duly confirmed in writing to the Disclosing Party or its proxy by the Receiving Part y.
perturbation.justification: The original text limited the confidentiality obligations to three years after the effective date. The changed text removes this limitation, making the obligation perpetual. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which requires the Receiving Party to destroy or return confidential information upon termination of the agreement. A perpetual obligation to maintain confidentiality is fundamentally incompatible with the instruction to destroy the information.

perturbation.original_text: We hereby acknowledge that the Company's bonds are listed for trading in the Tel:Aviv Stock Exchange, and therefore the Confidential Informatio n or any part thereof might be considered as Inside Information as this term is defined by the S ecurities Act of 1968 ) : ח"תשכ, חוק ניירות ערך 1968 ( . We hereby declare that as of the date hereunder we do not hold any bonds or other securities of the Company, and we undertake not to acquire any bonds or other securities of the Company within three months after receiving Confidential Informati on from you or the Company or anyone on your behalf. In addition, we undertake not to provide Co nfidential Information to anyone holding securities of the Company and/or anyone who might c onsider any transaction related to securities of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: We hereby acknowledge that the Company's bonds are listed for trading in the Tel:Aviv Stock Exchange, and therefore the Confidential Informatio n or any part thereof might be considered as Inside Information as this term is defined by the S ecurities Act of 1968 ) : ח"תשכ, חוק ניירות ערך 1968 ( . We hereby declare that as of the date hereunder we do not hold any bonds or other securities of the Company. In addition, we undertake not to provide Co nfidential Information to anyone holding securities of the Company and/or anyone who might c onsider any transaction related to securities of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the undertaking 'not to acquire any bonds or other securities of the Company within three months after receiving Confidential Information', this creates a potential conflict of interest. While the Receiving Party still agrees not to provide confidential information to those who might trade on it, the removal of the restriction on their *own* trading creates an in-text contradiction with the spirit of Section 9, which aims to prevent insider trading, and with Section 6's requirement to preserve the information's confidentiality and prevents trading based on it.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Inside Information We hereby acknowledge that the Company's bonds are listed for trading in the Tel:Aviv Stock Exchange, and therefore the Confidential Informatio n or any part thereof might be considered as Inside Information as this term is defined by the S ecurities Act of 1968 ) : ח"תשכ, חוק ניירות ערך 1968 ( . We hereby declare that as of the date hereunder we do not hold any bonds or other securities of the Company. In addition, we undertake not to provide Co nfidential Information to anyone holding securities of the Company and/or anyone who might c onsider any transaction related to securities of the Company.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that the Receiving Party would not acquire any bonds or other securities of the Company within three months after receiving Confidential Information. The changed text removes this restriction. The Contradicted Text states that this acknowledgement is made to adhere to the Securities Act of 1968. Removing the restriction creates a contradiction because the modified text allows the Receiving Party to potentially trade on inside information, which is what the original clause and the contradicted text were attempting to prevent. This creates a conflict of interest. This omission creates a contradiction with the spirit of preventing insider trading.
